steps.title: Analyze the sentence structure
steps.content:
The sentence reads: “...two other Black Americans who served in ______ Ralph Harold Metcalfe of Illinois and Alton R. Waldon Jr. of New York.” The clause before the blank is complete and independent. The names that follow are a list that specifies which Black Americans served in Congress. A colon is used to introduce a list or elaboration after a complete clause; a semicolon requires an independent clause on both sides; a period would strand the names as a fragment; no punctuation would fuse the list incoherently into the preceding clause.
  • Choice A — ‘Congress:’: Colon correctly introduces the list of names that specify who served in Congress. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Congress;’: Semicolons join two independent clauses; “Ralph Harold Metcalfe...and Alton R. Waldon Jr.” is a noun phrase list, not a clause. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Congress.’: A period ends the sentence and leaves the names as a fragment with no main verb. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Congress’: No punctuation makes the names appear to be the direct object of “served in,” which is grammatically incoherent. ✗

steps.title: Select the matching choice
steps.content: The colon is the only punctuation mark that correctly introduces a list elaborating on the preceding complete clause. That matches choice A — A is correct.
